question: Identify the root cause of the network fault: which device, what changed, and why it breaks.
description:
R3 re-points R4 from the RR-CLIENT neighbor-group to RR-PEER. R3 no
longer treats R4 as a client, so R3 stops reflecting R4-originated
prefixes to R2 and R6. R4 still reaches R7 (secondary RR, which keeps
R4 as RR-CLIENT), so prefix flow survives via R7. The asymmetry is
subtle and silently halves redundancy: if R7 is rebooted, R4's prefixes
vanish from the AS.
expected_rca:
Root cause: on 100.64.0.103 (R3, primary RR), the R4 neighbor was moved from the RR-CLIENT neighbor-group to RR-PEER, so R3 no longer treats R4 as a route-reflector client.
A route reflector only re-advertises prefixes learned from its clients, so once R4 is a plain peer R3 stops reflecting R4-originated prefixes to its other clients (R2, R6); R4 still reaches R7 (secondary RR, which keeps R4 as a client), so prefix flow survives but redundancy is silently halved - if R7 bounces, R4's prefixes vanish from the AS.
Fix: move R4 back to the RR-CLIENT neighbor-group on R3.
expected_symptoms_summary:
BGP session state change (%ROUTING-BGP-5-ADJCHANGE):
- R2: neighbor 192.0.2.3.
- R3: neighbor 192.0.2.4.
- R3: neighbor 192.0.2.4.

apply.commands:
configure terminal
router bgp 65000
neighbor 192.0.2.4
 no use neighbor-group RR-CLIENT
 use neighbor-group RR-PEER

revert.commands:
configure terminal
router bgp 65000
neighbor 192.0.2.4
 no use neighbor-group RR-PEER
 use neighbor-group RR-CLIENT

expected_symptoms: RP/0/RP0/CPU0:May 17 13:31:10.746 UTC: bgp[1084]: %ROUTING-BGP-5-ADJCHANGE : neighbor 192.0.2.3 Up (VRF: default) (AS: 65000)
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:May 17 13:30:16.534 UTC: bgp[1084]: %ROUTING-BGP-5-ADJCHANGE : neighbor 192.0.2.4 Down - RR client configuration changed (VRF: default) (AS: 65000)
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:May 17 13:30:21.320 UTC: bgp[1084]: %ROUTING-BGP-5-ADJCHANGE : neighbor 192.0.2.4 Up (VRF: default) (AS: 65000)
